name: Do et al. Misconception Faithfulness and Model-Limit Extract
population_or_context: Mathematics misconception simulation using Malrule arithmetic problems and EEDI multiple-choice misconception labels.
sample_size: Seven instruction-tuned LLMs spanning 4B-120B parameters; Malrule original train/test 790/210 and EEDI original train/test 800/200; synthetic training sets of 7,096 and 6,038 instances are reported for post-training.
methods: The study prompts LLMs to role-play students with an initial wrong answer caused by a latent misconception, then evaluates responses to targeted, misaligned, and generic feedback using Selective Flip Score. It compares base and reflective prompting and then SFT, DPO, and GRPO post-training pipelines.
education_context: Mathematics misconception simulation for AI tutor and educator-training contexts.
response_origin: LLMs generate simulated student replies to teacher feedback after being assigned an initial wrong answer and latent misconception context.
rubric_label_space: Selective belief-update behavior rather than a scoring rubric; response categories include correct flips and other feedback-response behaviors.
label_source: Misconception labels come from Malrule and EEDI; targeted, misaligned, and generic feedback conditions are constructed by the authors, with feedback generated under shared constraints.
generation_model: Llama 3.1 8B Instruct, Llama 3.3 70B Instruct, Qwen3 4B, Qwen3 80B Instruct, Qwen3 80B Thinking, GPT-OSS 20B, and GPT-OSS 120B.
generation_method: Prompted simulated-student role play under base and reflective prompts, followed by post-training experiments using SFT, DPO, and GRPO for selected models.
prompt_strategy: Base prompt specifies persona and initial wrong answer; reflective prompt asks the model to consider whether feedback is relevant to its reasoning.
evaluation_method: Selective Flip Score compares answer-flip rates under targeted feedback versus misaligned and generic controls, with qualitative response taxonomy and post-training comparisons.
scorer_use: Student-simulator fidelity benchmarking; not automated scorer validation.
real_response_comparator: Uses structured misconception datasets and EEDI real-world item/misconception labels; does not compare generated open responses against human-scored constructed responses.
reported_metrics: Table 3 reports near-zero SFS across models, datasets, and prompting strategies; examples include Qwen3-80B Instruct SFS of +0.01 across listed cells and GPT-OSS-120B SFS from +0.00 to +0.02. SFT improves SFS by +0.555 on Malrule and +0.250 on EEDI for Qwen3-4B, and by +0.447 on Malrule and +0.090 on EEDI for Llama3-8B.
limitations: The evaluation focuses on mathematics domains with structured misconception taxonomies.
Teacher feedback and response classifications are generated with LLM support for scalable evaluation.
SFS measures observable behavior, not latent belief states directly.
Post-training evidence should not be interpreted as an actionable recommendation for this project unless first-hand model training becomes in scope.
